class_schedule.topic: The Entrepreneurial Mindset and Opportunity Recognition
learning_objectives: Define entrepreneurship and the entrepreneurial process
Identify and evaluate business opportunities using lean methods
Distinguish between ideas and viable opportunities
readings: Blank, Why the Lean Start-Up Changes Everything (HBR)

lecture_notes.introduction: Entrepreneurship is the process of creating value by bringing together resources to exploit an opportunity. This course takes you from idea to viable business model through a hands-on, lean startup approach.
key_concepts: Entrepreneurial process: opportunity, resources, team, deal
Lean startup methodology: build-measure-learn cycle
Opportunity recognition: problem-solution fit
Customer discovery vs. customer validation
Entrepreneurial mindset: effectuation vs. causation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Rapid opportunity assessment: evaluating 5 business ideas against market size, feasibility, and founder-market fit criteria

review.value: Build a minimum viable product, test it with real customers, learn from feedback, and iterate; reduces waste and speeds learning
Confirming that the problem you identified actually exists and that your proposed solution addresses it; prerequisite for product-market fit

learning_objectives: Apply the Business Model Canvas to design business models
Identify and test key assumptions in a business model

lecture_notes.introduction: A business model describes how a company creates, delivers, and captures value. The Business Model Canvas provides a structured way to map and test the nine building blocks of any business.
key_concepts: Nine blocks: customer segments, value propositions, channels, relationships, revenue streams, key resources, key activities, key partnerships, cost structure
Hypothesis-driven entrepreneurship: map assumptions, rank by risk, test cheaply
Minimum Viable Product (MVP) strategies
Pivoting: when and how to change direction
Business model patterns: freemium, marketplace, razor-and-blade, subscription
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building and stress-testing a Business Model Canvas for a real startup idea, identifying the riskiest assumptions
setup_requirements: Business Model Canvas poster or digital tool

review.value: A strategic management tool that visualizes the nine building blocks of a business model on a single page
Minimum Viable Product: the simplest version of the product that allows you to start the build-measure-learn cycle with real customers

learning_objectives: Validate market demand before building product

lecture_notes.introduction: The biggest risk in entrepreneurship is building something nobody wants. Customer discovery helps you validate demand before investing heavily in product development.
key_concepts: The Mom Test: asking questions that give reliable data instead of compliments
Customer interview techniques: open-ended, behavior-focused, no pitching
Customer personas and jobs-to-be-done framework
Market sizing: TAM, SAM, SOM
Landing page tests and smoke tests for demand validation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mock customer interviews: practicing interview techniques and extracting actionable insights from conversations

review.value: Talk about their life, not your idea; ask about specifics in the past, not generics about the future; talk less, listen more
Total Addressable Market, Serviceable Addressable Market, Serviceable Obtainable Market: nested market sizing from theoretical maximum to realistic initial target

learning_objectives: Design revenue models appropriate to the business type
Build startup financial projections: P&L, cash flow, runway
Understand unit economics and the path to profitability

lecture_notes.introduction: Revenue models determine how your business captures the value it creates. Financial projections translate your business model into numbers that guide decisions and attract investors.
key_concepts: Revenue models: subscription, transaction, licensing, advertising, marketplace fees
Unit economics: CAC, LTV, contribution margin, payback period
Startup financial projections: bottom-up vs. top-down approach
Cash flow management and runway calculation
Burn rate and when to fundraise
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a 12-month financial model for a SaaS startup, calculating unit economics and runway under different growth scenarios

review.value: Revenue and cost per customer unit; if LTV > 3x CAC with payback under 12 months, the business model is typically investable
Cash on hand divided by monthly burn rate; the number of months until the company runs out of money

learning_objectives: Compare funding options: bootstrapping, angels, VCs, grants, crowdfunding
Understand term sheets and equity dilution

lecture_notes.introduction: Funding is a means to an end, not an end itself. Understanding when and how to raise capital, and on what terms, is critical for maintaining control and maximizing long-term value.
key_concepts: Bootstrapping: funding through revenue and personal savings
Angel investors and seed funding: typical terms and expectations
Venture capital: stages (pre-seed through Series C+), valuation, dilution
Pitch deck structure: problem, solution, market, traction, team, ask
Term sheet essentials: valuation, liquidation preferences, board seats, vesting
lecture_notes.demonstration: Pitch practice: each team delivers a 5-minute pitch and receives structured feedback from mock investors

review.value: Each funding round reduces founders' ownership percentage; raising at higher valuations minimizes dilution but must be justified by traction
Investors get their money back before common shareholders in an exit; participating preferred means they get their money back AND share in remaining proceeds

class_schedule.topic: Scaling, Growth, and Building the Team
learning_objectives: Identify inflection points that signal readiness to scale
Design organizational structures for growth-stage startups
Build and manage founding teams and early hires
readings: Horowitz, The Hard Thing About Hard Things (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Scaling a startup is fundamentally different from starting one. The skills, processes, and culture that got you to product-market fit must evolve to support rapid growth.
key_concepts: Product-market fit signals: retention, organic growth, pull vs. push
Growth channels: viral, paid, content, sales, partnerships
Scaling operations: processes, systems, and delegation
Founding team dynamics: equity splits, roles, conflict resolution
Hiring strategy: generalists early, specialists later
lecture_notes.demonstration: Growth strategy workshop: designing a customer acquisition engine with multiple channels and measuring CAC per channel

review.value: The point where the market pulls the product from you; measured by retention rates, organic referrals, and willingness to pay
Sticky (high retention), Viral (users bring users), Paid (CAC < LTV); most successful startups rely on one primary engine

questions.question: A founder says: 'I asked 50 people if they would use my app and 45 said yes.' Explain why this data is unreliable using The Mom Test principles, and describe how the founder should have structured the interviews.
questions.answer: This data is unreliable because people say they would use products to be polite, not because they actually would. The Mom Test teaches: (1) Never ask if someone would use your product; instead ask about their current behavior and pain points. (2) Ask about specifics in the past: 'Tell me about the last time you faced this problem. What did you do?' (3) Look for commitment signals: 'Would you sign up for our beta right now?' or 'Would you pay $X today?' Words are cheap; actions and past behavior are reliable data. The founder should have asked about the problem, not the solution, and sought evidence of urgency and willingness to pay.

rubric.key: Identifies why survey data is unreliable

questions.question: What is the difference between a pivot and a failure? Give an example of a famous startup pivot.
questions.answer: A pivot is a structured course correction based on validated learning; a failure is running out of resources or giving up without applying lessons. A pivot changes one element of the business model (customer segment, value proposition, channel, revenue model) while preserving what has been learned. Famous example: Slack started as Tiny Speck, a gaming company building a game called Glitch. The game failed, but the internal communication tool they built for their team had strong product-market fit. They pivoted from gaming to enterprise communication, becoming one of the fastest-growing SaaS companies. The pivot preserved their technology expertise while changing the customer and value proposition.

rubric.key: Clear distinction between pivot and failure

questions.question: A SaaS startup has CAC of $500, monthly subscription of $50, and average customer lifetime of 24 months. Calculate LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, and payback period. Is this business model healthy?
questions.answer: LTV = $50/month x 24 months = $1,200. LTV:CAC ratio = $1,200 / $500 = 2.4x. Payback period = $500 / $50/month = 10 months. Assessment: The LTV:CAC of 2.4x is below the ideal 3:1 benchmark, suggesting the company either needs to reduce CAC, increase prices, improve retention, or find upsell opportunities. The 10-month payback period is acceptable but ties up capital. The business model is viable but needs improvement to be attractive to investors.

questions.question: A startup raises $2M at a $8M pre-money valuation. What percentage of the company do the investors own? If the founders previously owned 100%, what is their post-round ownership?
questions.answer: Post-money valuation = Pre-money + Investment = $8M + $2M = $10M. Investor ownership = $2M / $10M = 20%. Founder post-round ownership = 100% - 20% = 80%. The founders have been diluted from 100% to 80%, giving up 20% of the company in exchange for $2M in capital to grow the business.

major_assignments.title: Startup Launch Project
major_assignments.description: Develop a real startup concept from idea through validated business model. Conduct customer discovery interviews, build and test an MVP or landing page, develop financial projections, and pitch to a panel of guest judges.
objectives: Validate a business opportunity through customer discovery
Build and test a minimum viable product or landing page
Develop realistic financial projections with unit economics
deliverables: Business Model Canvas with validation evidence
MVP or validated landing page with traffic data

criteria: All nine Canvas blocks completed with evidence
Team credibility and ask clearly stated
Thorough customer discovery with documented evidence
Validated business model with clear value proposition
Realistic financial projections with unit economics

class_participation.description: Active engagement in workshops, pitch practice, and peer feedback

major_assignments.description: Startup launch project spanning the full semester

midterm_examination.description: Covers lean startup, customer discovery, and business model design

final_pitch.description: Final pitch to panel of guest judges (entrepreneurs and investors)

course_policies.attendance: Mandatory attendance for workshop sessions and pitch practices. Guest speaker sessions are especially valuable.
course_policies.late_submissions: Customer discovery milestones have firm deadlines. Late pitch decks receive 20% penalty per day.
course_policies.collaboration: Startup project may be completed in teams of 2-4. All team members must participate in the final pitch.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Customer interviews must be real (not fabricated). Landing page data must be authentic. Fabricating traction data is grounds for course failure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Landing page builder, financial modeling in Excel, pitch deck software. Access to customer interview recording tools.
